answer:
Three couplets where 'dard' is doing different work, with what an English-only reader misses each time:

1. Ghalib (Diwan-E-Ghalib, ghazal 209, p-39e8e8). 'Dard-e-dil likhoon kab tak' — translated as: 'How long to write this pain? I must combine, my own, / My wounded fingers with this bloody line, my own.' Here dard is wound-as-script: the literal blood of a cut finger continues to flow until it has done the work of the pen. 'Pain' loses the fact that dard-e-dil is itself a recognised genre-marker (the lover's writ) — and the very thing that licences blood as ink. The English reader sees a metaphor; the Urdu reader sees a genre-conscious poet executing it.

2. Iqbal (Bang-e-Dara Part 1, ghazal 2, p-4466e4). 'Ilaaj-e-dard mein bhi dard ki lazzat pe marta hoon' — translated as: 'In treating anguish, I embrace its pain.' Dard here is what one is dying-for-the-taste-of (lazzat). The cure is itself a reason to relish the malady. 'Pain' loses the insistence that dard has flavour, that it is something one can be a connoisseur of (dard-mand = one who has tasted dard, a sympathiser-by-experience). The English flattens an aesthetic doctrine into a contradiction.

3. Zauq (Diwan-E-Zauq, ghazal 30, p-1a2c4f). 'Miri haqeeqat-e-pur-dard ko kabhi uss se / ba-aah-o-naala na kahwe, ba-chashm-e-tar to kahe' — translated as: 'Not with loud sighs that mimic sorrow's rain, / But with wet eyes is true expression.' Here dard is the substance of one's own truth (haqeeqat-e-pur-dard = the dard-saturated truth of who one is); the issue is not whether to express dard but how — the chashm-e-tar (wet eye) being the licit register, the loud aah-o-naala the illicit one. 'Sorrow' loses the claim that dard is identity, not affect — and that there is a Mughal-court etiquette governing how it ought to be borne.

What 'pain' loses across all three: dard is (a) a genre Ghalib writes inside, (b) a flavour Iqbal cultivates, (c) the constitutive matter of selfhood Zauq calibrates the etiquette for. English 'pain' makes all three sound like the same affect.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: p-1a2c4f = the translation that flattens 'pur-dard' to 'sorrow'
